summary: Gruber argues that the term 'podcast' should be reserved for audio shows distributed via open RSS feeds, playable in any client — not proprietary audio locked to a single app. He uses Luminary's $100 million launch as the catalyst, noting the company occupies an awkward position: producing proprietary ad-free shows while also serving as a podcast player that can't even play several major podcasts. He draws analogies to websites and email to argue that client-agnosticism is definitional to podcasting. He further argues Luminary's Netflix analogy is fatally flawed because podcast content is cheap to produce and freely available, undermining both sides of Netflix's value proposition. He predicts the $100 million in venture capital will be wasted.
key_points: A podcast is defined by its open distribution via RSS feeds playable in any client, just as a website is defined by being viewable in any browser.
Luminary occupies a contradictory position: promoting ad-free proprietary shows while depending on ad-supported open podcasts for its catalog, yet missing major shows other players can access.
The 'Netflix of audio' analogy fails because podcast content is cheap to produce and the non-original catalog is freely available to any app, unlike Netflix's licensed content.
Spotify and Luminary are trying to usurp the word 'podcast' because people love podcasts, but the openness itself is part of what people love.
Luminary's app doesn't even allow adding podcasts by RSS feed URL, making it a fundamentally limited podcast player.
money_quotes: So is a podcast a 'podcast' if it only works in one app? I'm going to say no.
All podcasts are shows on the internet, but not all shows on the internet are podcasts.

These companies are trying to usurp the word podcast for one simple reason: people love podcasts. What I think and hope they are missing is that part of what people love about podcasts is the openness.
It's one of the last remaining areas of the internet that works exactly as the internet was intended to work.
The test is not in the execution. The test is whether it makes any sense at all to try to be the 'Netflix of audio shows'.
40 engineers seems cuckoo, not just for this podcast app but for any podcast app.
This is like a web browser not allowing you to just paste in a URL, allowing you only to get to pages through a search engine.
key_insight: Podcasting's defining characteristic is its openness — an audio show locked to a single app is just a show, not a podcast — and companies trying to capture the term while abandoning the openness are missing that the openness itself is the point.
